title: Nvidia wants to cut data center water use, but that’s not the same as fixing AI’s water problem
slides: Nvidia cuts water use, so why isn't AI's thirst solved?
Its new cooling needs 20% less water per chip.
But most AI water comes from training, not cooling.
Your cloud provider still drowns in hidden consumption.
Before celebrating, check where your model trains.
thread_text:
1. Still thirsty?

Nvidia's new cooling cuts water by 20% per chip, but training a single large model can consume millions of liters.

2. Where the real gulps happen

Up to 90% of AI's water footprint is from training, not keeping servers cool.

3. Cloud's hidden drain

Your cloud provider doesn't show you the water sucked by data centers for model training, only bills.

4. The gap in the fix

Cooling efficiency is a drop—no one's tackled the training water cost at scale yet.

5. Check your training site

Before you deploy, ask your cloud provider for the water usage report for your specific model run.
video_script: Most AI water is for training.
Ask your provider for water data.

script: Most AI water is for training.
Ask your provider for water data.

instagram.caption:
Nvidia’s new cooling tech uses 20% less water per chip, but training a single AI model can gulp millions of liters.

That 20% saving only affects the tiny fraction of water used for cooling. Up to 90% of AI’s water footprint is burned during training itself — and your cloud provider gives you no visibility into that.

If you train or deploy models, your actual water cost is invisible in the bill.

Before your next run, have you asked your cloud provider for the specific water report for that training job? 💧
